item.title: Trump votes by mail as he dubs mail-in voting ‘cheating’
item.description: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump took advantage of mail-in voting this week in a special election in Florida, even as he criticized mail-in ballots as “cheating” in his push for national legislation to enact new regulations on voting.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump took advantage of mail-in voting this week in a special election in Florida, even as he criticized mail-in ballots as “cheating” in his push for national legislation to enact new regulations on voting.

item.title: Trump’s approval hits new 36% low as fuel prices surge amid Iran war, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump’s approval rating fell in recent days to its lowest point since he returned to the White House, hit by a surge in fuel prices and widespread disapproval of the war he launched on Iran, a Reuters/Ipsos poll found.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — President Donald Trump’s approval rating fell in recent days to its lowest point since he returned to the White House, hit by a surge in fuel prices and widespread disapproval of the war he launched on Iran, a Reuters/Ipsos poll found.

item.title: US safety agency says tracking system failed at LaGuardia jet collision
item.description: NEW YORK/MONTREAL — The National Transportation Safety Board said on Tuesday that a system which would have allowed a New York airport controller to track movement of aircraft and vehicles did not alert during a Sunday night collision between an Air Canada commercial jet and a truck that killed two pilots.
item.source:markdown: NEW YORK/MONTREAL — The National Transportation Safety Board said on Tuesday that a system which would have allowed a New York airport controller to track movement of aircraft and vehicles did not alert during a Sunday night collision between an Air Canada commercial jet and a truck that killed two pilots.

item.title: TSA says 460 airport officers quit as standoff poses major security risks
item.description: The Homeland Security Department will tell Congress today that a funding standoff that has forced 50,000 airport security officers to go without pay has caused delays at airports and is posing major security risks.
item.source:markdown: The Homeland Security Department will tell Congress today that a funding standoff that has forced 50,000 airport security officers to go without pay has caused delays at airports and is posing major security risks.

item.title: Minnesota prosecutors sue for evidence in shootings by immigration agents
item.description: Law enforcement officials in Minnesota sued the federal government on Tuesday, seeking evidence that could be used to prosecute agents involved in three separate shootings during an immigration enforcement crackdown this year.
item.source:markdown: Law enforcement officials in Minnesota sued the federal government on Tuesday, seeking evidence that could be used to prosecute agents involved in three separate shootings during an immigration enforcement crackdown this year.

item.title: Trump and RNC lean toward Dallas for an unusual midterm convention
item.description: President Donald Trump and the Republican National Committee are strongly considering Dallas as the site of an unusual midterm convention later this year, according to two people with knowledge of the discussions. Republicans are working to finalize the plan as they seek to rally their voters in the face of political headwinds.
item.source:markdown: President Donald Trump and the Republican National Committee are strongly considering Dallas as the site of an unusual midterm convention later this year, according to two people with knowledge of the discussions. Republicans are working to finalize the plan as they seek to rally their voters in the face of political headwinds.

item.title: Traders bet $500 million on oil price just before Trump’s post on delay to Iran attack
item.description: LONDON — Traders bet half a billion dollars on the price of crude only 15 minutes before U.S. President Donald Trump announced a five-day delay to attacks on Iran’s energy infrastructure that sent the market plunging, exchange data and Reuters calculations showed.
item.source:markdown: LONDON — Traders bet half a billion dollars on the price of crude only 15 minutes before U.S. President Donald Trump announced a five-day delay to attacks on Iran’s energy infrastructure that sent the market plunging, exchange data and Reuters calculations showed.

item.title: FBI investigation into Kash Patel was more extensive than previously reported
item.description: WASHINGTON — A special counsel investigation that began in 2022 of Kash Patel, who is now the FBI director but was then a private citizen, included demands for more than two years of phone records, text messages and financial information, according to two grand jury subpoenas and nondisclosure orders viewed by Reuters.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — A special counsel investigation that began in 2022 of Kash Patel, who is now the FBI director but was then a private citizen, included demands for more than two years of phone records, text messages and financial information, according to two grand jury subpoenas and nondisclosure orders viewed by Reuters.

item.title: Micah Gurr accepted to Air Force Academy Preparatory School
item.description:
Source: Congresswoman Uifa’atali Amata’s D.C. staff

Washington, D.C. — Congresswoman Uifa’atali Amata is congratulating Micah Tiamanini I’atala Gurr for his acceptance to the U.S. Air Force Academy Preparatory School in Colorado Springs.

The mission of the Preparatory School, established in 1961, is to provide a rigorous ten-month program refining the academic, leadership, and physical skills to prepare cadet candidates for success as future officers. Admission is selective and competitive. Numerous USAFA Preparatory School graduates have gone on to distinguished military and academic careers and honors, including prestigious scholarships, fellowships, general officer ranks, and even astronauts.

Cadet candidates who were not already enlisted at the time of their acceptance are placed on active duty in the Air Force Reserves during the program.

“Congratulations to Micah Gurr on this acceptance. Micah has an exciting year in store for him, then we look forward to another announcement in a year updating his next step,” Congresswoman Amata.  “Only excellent students get this opportunity, as the Service Academies look for important marks of leadership and motivation to qualify for Preparatory School. Congratulations to his proud parents, Malaeoletalu and Shantel Gurr, as well as his teachers, and Samoana High School.”

A Service Academy preparatory school requires strong academics, high test scores, and the demonstration of extracurricular activities, volunteering, or other roles that show leadership and motivation.

Congresswoman Amata nominated Micah this January among a group of six student nominees who sent in nine total Service Academy applications this year from American Samoa. He is the second announcement from this class of nominees.

Service Academy Application Information

Congresswoman Amata’s Chief of Staff, Col. Leafaina Tavai, U.S. Army (Ret.), who was the first service academy graduate from American Samoa, graduating from the U.S. Military Academy, recommends that motivated students consider applying to multiple Academies, and notes that students cannot be nominated by the Congresswoman without a completed application on file with the Academy ahead of the congressional nomination.

The Service Academies are selective and highly competitive. Prospective applicants should evaluate their interest in five total Service Academy possibilities: The U.S. Military Academy at West Point, the U.S. Naval Academy in Annapolis, the U.S. Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs, the U.S. Merchant Marine Academy at Kings Point, New York, and the U.S. Coast Guard Academy in New London, Connecticut. The Coast Guard Academy does not include a congressional nomination process, but the Congresswoman will be very happy to highlight and congratulate every Academy acceptance, so American Samoa can take pride in each of these high-achieving students.

 

 

Section: 
Local News
Tags: 
U.S. Air Force Academy Preparatory School
item.source:markdown:
[![MICAH GURR](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/slideshow/public/field/image/micah_gurr_usafa_prep_school_0.jpg?itok=sc4q-If4)](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/article_normal_image/public/field/image/micah_gurr_usafa_prep_school_0.jpg?itok=3v0bVJE1)
Source: Congresswoman Uifa’atali Amata’s D.C. staff
Washington, D.C. — Congresswoman Uifa’atali Amata is congratulating Micah Tiamanini I’atala Gurr for his acceptance to the U.S. Air Force Academy Preparatory School in Colorado Springs.
The mission of the Preparatory School, established in 1961, is to provide a rigorous ten-month program refining the academic, leadership, and physical skills to prepare cadet candidates for success as future officers. Admission is selective and competitive. Numerous USAFA Preparatory School graduates have gone on to distinguished military and academic careers and honors, including prestigious scholarships, fellowships, general officer ranks, and even astronauts.
Cadet candidates who were not already enlisted at the time of their acceptance are placed on active duty in the Air Force Reserves during the program.
“Congratulations to Micah Gurr on this acceptance. Micah has an exciting year in store for him, then we look forward to another announcement in a year updating his next step,” Congresswoman Amata. “Only excellent students get this opportunity, as the Service Academies look for important marks of leadership and motivation to qualify for Preparatory School. Congratulations to his proud parents, Malaeoletalu and Shantel Gurr, as well as his teachers, and Samoana High School.”
A Service Academy preparatory school requires strong academics, high test scores, and the demonstration of extracurricular activities, volunteering, or other roles that show leadership and motivation.
Congresswoman Amata nominated Micah this January among a group of six student nominees who sent in nine total Service Academy applications this year from American Samoa. He is the second announcement from this class of nominees.
Service Academy Application Information
Congresswoman Amata’s Chief of Staff, Col. Leafaina Tavai, U.S. Army (Ret.), who was the first service academy graduate from American Samoa, graduating from the U.S. Military Academy, recommends that motivated students consider applying to multiple Academies, and notes that students cannot be nominated by the Congresswoman without a completed application on file with the Academy ahead of the congressional nomination.
The Service Academies are selective and highly competitive. Prospective applicants should evaluate their interest in five total Service Academy possibilities: The U.S. Military Academy at West Point, the U.S. Naval Academy in Annapolis, the U.S. Air Force Academy in Colorado Springs, the U.S. Merchant Marine Academy at Kings Point, New York, and the U.S. Coast Guard Academy in New London, Connecticut. The Coast Guard Academy does not include a congressional nomination process, but the Congresswoman will be very happy to highlight and congratulate every Academy acceptance, so American Samoa can take pride in each of these high-achieving students.

item.description: Petrona G. Harrell, 72, of Kahaluu, Oahu, died March 19 at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. She was born in Kahaluu. Visitation 11-11:30 a.m. Wednesday (March 25) at Homelani Memorial Park. Service at 11:30 a.m. Cremation to follow. Online condolences: homelanimemorialpark.com. Survived by husband, Eddie Wallace Harrell; children, Tina (William) Primacio, Keoni (Aloha) Tsukiyama, Kapono (Krsangi) Tsukiyama, Eddie Boy (Summer) Harrell and Shane (Erin) Harrell; brothers, Ronald (Dora) Gonzalez and Don Manthei; sisters, Leslie (Kurt) Cazinha, Oveta Gonzalez and Lucia Gonzalez; 17 grandchildren and 11 great‑grandchildren. Arrangements by Homelani Memorial Park.

item.title: Trump approved Iran operation after Netanyahu argued for joint killing of Khamenei, sources say
item.description: WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM — Less than 48 hours before the U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran began, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu spoke by phone to President Donald Trump about the reasons for launching the kind of complex, far-off war the American leader once had campaigned against. Both Trump and Netanyahu knew from intelligence briefings earlier in the week that Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his key lieutenants would soon meet at his compound in Tehran, making them vulnerable to a “decapitation strike” — an attack against a country’s top leaders often used by Israelis but traditionally less so by the United States.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM — Less than 48 hours before the U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran began, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu spoke by phone to President Donald Trump about the reasons for launching the kind of complex, far-off war the American leader once had campaigned against. Both Trump and Netanyahu knew from intelligence briefings earlier in the week that Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his key lieutenants would soon meet at his compound in Tehran, making them vulnerable to a “decapitation strike” — an attack against a country’s top leaders often used by Israelis but traditionally less so by the United States.

item.title: California sues US Dept of Energy over Sable oil pipeline restart
item.description: California Attorney General Rob Bonta said on Monday he has sued the U.S. Department of Energy to stop it from using a Cold War-era law to restart the long-disputed Sable Offshore pipeline system linking the Santa Ynez offshore platform to California refineries. U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright earlier this month restarted the pipelines using powers granted to him by President Donald Trump through an executive order that invoked the Defense Production Act to supersede state laws.

item.title: Hello? Wake up American Samoa
item.description:
Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA — It is trending on our social media platforms — Rose Atoll. You say opening Rose Atoll is beneficial to our economy. We have to be more realistic. Are we really choosing money over our environment?

If we go and fish there, it will hurt nature and the environment. It could damage the coral and the ocean habitat. We are responsible for ensuring that the Environmental Species Act keeps our endangered species, like turtles safe. That’s our responsibility as the Samoan people. We are the caretakers of our ocean and land. Commercial fishing vessels threaten our turtles by catching them. It may not be on purpose but it happens.

This cannot be. Dear leaders of American Samoa, please protect Rose Atoll.

 

 

Section: 
Local News
Tags: 
ROSE ATOLL
item.source:markdown:
[![I HEART TURTLES](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/slideshow/public/field/image/_i_heart_turtles.jpg?itok=UlhCY5BC)](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/article_normal_image/public/field/image/_i_heart_turtles.jpg?itok=NA4cX31q)
![](data:image/png;base64,)
_Pago Pago, AMERICAN SAMOA —_ It is trending on our social media platforms — Rose Atoll. You say opening Rose Atoll is beneficial to our economy. We have to be more realistic. Are we really choosing money over our environment?
If we go and fish there, it will hurt nature and the environment. It could damage the coral and the ocean habitat. We are responsible for ensuring that the Environmental Species Act keeps our endangered species, like turtles safe. That’s our responsibility as the Samoan people. We are the caretakers of our ocean and land. Commercial fishing vessels threaten our turtles by catching them. It may not be on purpose but it happens.
This cannot be. Dear leaders of American Samoa, please protect Rose Atoll.

item.title: WestPac Council to consider restoring fishing in Pacific Marine Monuments
item.description:
Honolulu, HI — The Western Pacific Regional Fishery Management Council will deliberate on two major actions at its 206th meeting this week allowing U.S. fishermen to fish in U.S. Pacific marine national monuments.

The first action concerns the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument expansion area around Wake and Jarvis Islands, and Johnston Atoll.

The Council will take final action on managing commercial fishing from 50-to-200 nm following Presidential Proclamation 10918 (April 17, 2025). The Proclamation directed the Secretary of Commerce to revise regulations to allow appropriately managed U.S. commercial fishing in those waters. U.S. longline and purse seine vessels would be allowed to fish in these areas following strict federal fishery requirements. These include permits, catch limits, gear restrictions, logbooks and observer coverage for monitoring catch and protected species interactions, and vessel monitoring systems for enforcing closed areas.

The second action involves the Papahānaumokuākea, Rose Atoll and Marianas Trench Marine National Monuments.

Executive Order (EO) 14276 (April 17, 2025) directed federal agencies to review monument fishing restrictions and recommend changes to support sustainable U.S. seafood production, while maintaining conservation objectives. The Council will also consider recommendations restoring regulated commercial fishing access under existing Magnuson-Stevens Act authorities as requested by the EO.

In making its decisions, the Council will determine the impacts of the alternatives on the affected environment, and consider recommendations from industry and science advisory bodies, and comments from the public. The meeting is expected to draw broad interest because the decisions sit at the intersection of fishing access, seafood security and marine conservation. In Hawaiʻi, for example, the Northwestern Hawaiian Islands fishery once provided nearly half of the bottomfish sold in the Hawaiʻi market before its closure in 2011.

Why Attend?

The Council meeting provides the public an opportunity to learn about proposed fishery actions, hear reports from around the region and provide input on decisions affecting fishing communities, local seafood supply and marine resource management across the U.S. Pacific Islands. 

How to Join - March 24-26, 2026

In-Person: Ala Moana Hotel, Hibiscus Ballroom (410 Atkinson St., Honolulu, HI)

Online: Join remotely via Webex: https://tinyurl.com/206CouncilMtg, Event password: CM206mtg

Get the Full Agenda & Documents: www.wpcouncil.org/event/206th-council-meeting

 

Section: 
Local News
Tags: 
Western Pacific Regional Fishery Management Council
item.source:markdown:
[![Marine Monument map](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/slideshow/public/field/image/marine_monument_map_.png?itok=k0H7g9N4)](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/article_normal_image/public/field/image/marine_monument_map_.png?itok=6mkzVZ5J)

item.title: Attack on a Sudan hospital kills dozens, head of WHO says

item.title: Musk says SpaceX and Tesla to build advanced chip factories in Austin
item.description: LOS ANGELES — SpaceX and Tesla will build two advanced chip factories at a sprawling facility in Austin, Texas, one to power cars and humanoid robots, and another designed for artificial intelligence data centers in space, CEO Elon Musk said on Sunday.

item.title: US Southwest heat wave tumbles records, raises fire risk

item.title: Cuba restores power to Havana after second grid collapse in a week
item.description: HAVANA — Cuba had restored power to nearly half of the capital Havana by Sunday afternoon, officials said, less than 24 hours after the national grid collapsed for the second time in a week amid a U.S. oil blockade that has dealt a major blow to the island’s already ailing energy infrastructure.
item.source:markdown: HAVANA — Cuba had restored power to nearly half of the capital Havana by Sunday afternoon, officials said, less than 24 hours after the national grid collapsed for the second time in a week amid a U.S. oil blockade that has dealt a major blow to the island’s already ailing energy infrastructure.

item.title: Steves: Sightseeing high and low in hilly Lyon
item.description: Smartly situated Lyon — just south of Burgundy, north of Provence, east of the Alps, and centered on a peninsula at the convergence of two major rivers — has long been a major culture hub and a foodie mecca. But its enjoyable cityscape is refreshingly untouristy, despite its classy shops, worthwhile museums, renowned cuisine, and a romantic old town of cobbled alleys and pastel Renaissance mansions. Just two hours from Paris by train, Lyon makes an easy one- or two-night stopover.
item.source:markdown: Smartly situated Lyon — just south of Burgundy, north of Provence, east of the Alps, and centered on a peninsula at the convergence of two major rivers — has long been a major culture hub and a foodie mecca. But its enjoyable cityscape is refreshingly untouristy, despite its classy shops, worthwhile museums, renowned cuisine, and a romantic old town of cobbled alleys and pastel Renaissance mansions. Just two hours from Paris by train, Lyon makes an easy one- or two-night stopover.

item.description: The summit region of Kilauea experienced tephra fallout and a Kona low storm this past week. Cleanup, recovery, and regrowth is ongoing. These processes are natural and have been recognized and embodied in Native Hawaiian oral traditions about Pele, Lono and Hi‘iaka.

item.title: Obituaries for March 22
item.description: Entiro “Terry” Binyan Atiz, 99, of Riverside, Calif., formerly of Mountain View, died Jan. 20 at home. He was a heavy equipment operator for Yamada and Sons Inc., former owner of Atiz Nursery and E&R Dozer service and member of the former Sampaguita Club. Visitation 9-11 a.m. Saturday (March 28) at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Memorial service at 11 a.m. Urn committal to follow at Mauna Kea Memorial Park in Papaikou. Survived by son, David (Claire) Atiz; daughters, Gloria (Danny) Lagunte, Clarita Atiz, Sharon Aoki, Donna (John) Ishii and Michele (Louis Tejeda) Atiz; 18 grandchildren and 24 great-grandchildren; nieces and nephews.Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.
item.source:markdown: Entiro “Terry” Binyan Atiz, 99, of Riverside, Calif., formerly of Mountain View, died Jan. 20 at home. He was a heavy equipment operator for Yamada and Sons Inc., former owner of Atiz Nursery and E&R Dozer service and member of the former Sampaguita Club. Visitation 9-11 a.m. Saturday (March 28) at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Memorial service at 11 a.m. Urn committal to follow at Mauna Kea Memorial Park in Papaikou. Survived by son, David (Claire) Atiz; daughters, Gloria (Danny) Lagunte, Clarita Atiz, Sharon Aoki, Donna (John) Ishii and Michele (Louis Tejeda) Atiz; 18 grandchildren and 24 great-grandchildren; nieces and nephews.Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

item.title: Trump threatens to put ICE agents in airports over funding impasse
item.description: WASHINGTON (Reuters) — U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to deploy Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to U.S. airports on Monday if congressional Democrats do not immediately agree to fund airport safety. “I will move our brilliant and patriotic ICE Agents to the Airports where they will do Security like no one has ever seen before,” Trump wrote on Truth Social on Saturday, more than a month into a partial government shutdown. Transportation Security Administration personnel are set to miss a second full paycheck on Fridayas a result of the shutdown as lawmakers clash over funding for the Department of Homeland Security, the parent agency for TSA and ICE.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON (Reuters) — U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to deploy Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to U.S. airports on Monday if congressional Democrats do not immediately agree to fund airport safety. “I will move our brilliant and patriotic ICE Agents to the Airports where they will do Security like no one has ever seen before,” Trump wrote on Truth Social on Saturday, more than a month into a partial government shutdown. Transportation Security Administration personnel are set to miss a second full paycheck on Fridayas a result of the shutdown as lawmakers clash over funding for the Department of Homeland Security, the parent agency for TSA and ICE.

item.title: Robert S. Mueller III, 81, dies; rebuilt FBI and led Trump inquiry
item.description: Robert S. Mueller III, who led the FBI for 12 tumultuous years, brought politically explosive indictments as a special counsel examining Russia’s attack on the 2016 presidential election, and then concluded that he could neither absolve nor accuse President Donald Trump of a crime, died Friday. He was 81.
item.source:markdown: Robert S. Mueller III, who led the FBI for 12 tumultuous years, brought politically explosive indictments as a special counsel examining Russia’s attack on the 2016 presidential election, and then concluded that he could neither absolve nor accuse President Donald Trump of a crime, died Friday. He was 81.

item.title: US attacks Iranian drones and vessels to try to open strategic shipping lane
item.description: JERUSALEM — U.S. warplanes and attack helicopters have ramped up assaults against Iranian drones and naval vessels, U.S. officials said, in an effort to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the strategic waterway that Iran has effectively closed to most Western ships. The de facto blockade has helped drive up oil prices and rocked the global economy.
item.source:markdown: JERUSALEM — U.S. warplanes and attack helicopters have ramped up assaults against Iranian drones and naval vessels, U.S. officials said, in an effort to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the strategic waterway that Iran has effectively closed to most Western ships. The de facto blockade has helped drive up oil prices and rocked the global economy.

item.title: Aid ship departs for Cuba as island grapples with a fuel blockade

item.title: Shigeaki Mori, Hiroshima survivor photographed with Obama, dies at 88
item.description: Shigeaki Mori, who survived the first American atomic bomb as a boy in Japan and then spent decades as an adult researching what happened to 12 American prisoners who were killed in the attack, an effort that led to a widely photographed, tearful encounter with President Barack Obama, died on Saturday in Hiroshima, Japan. He was 88.
item.source:markdown: Shigeaki Mori, who survived the first American atomic bomb as a boy in Japan and then spent decades as an adult researching what happened to 12 American prisoners who were killed in the attack, an effort that led to a widely photographed, tearful encounter with President Barack Obama, died on Saturday in Hiroshima, Japan. He was 88.

item.title: Rose Atoll: I cannot stand and just agree, that profit should rule land and sea
item.description:
By 
Fetutasi Misi

In the quiet blue of the endless sea,

There lies a place still wild and free,

A ring of coral, soft and bright,

Where waves whisper day and night.

Rose Atoll stands, untouched, alone,

A fragile world the ocean has grown,

With birds that soar and turtles that glide,

And secrets beneath the tide.

The government watches, careful and wise,

Protecting its beauty from greedy eyes,

They guard the land, the reefs, the shore,

So it can stay forevermore.

But some see money in its island,

They dream of fishing boats a plenty,

Turning peace into a crowded place,

Erasing nature without a trace.

I cannot stand and just agree,

That profit should rule land and sea,

For once it’s gone, we can’t restore,

The life that lived on that quiet shore.

Let Rose Atoll remain the same,

Not just a place, but more than a name,

A symbol of what we choose to save

Not everything is ours to pave.

 

Section: 
Local News
Tags: 
ROSE ATOLL
item.source:markdown:
[![Fesili Mai banner](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/slideshow/public/field/image/fesili_mai_march_banner.png?itok=VtuZRf1i)](https://www.samoanews.com/sites/default/files/styles/article_normal_image/public/field/image/fesili_mai_march_banner.png?itok=HQSztmms)
By
Fetutasi Misi
In the quiet blue of the endless sea,
There lies a place still wild and free,
A ring of coral, soft and bright,
Where waves whisper day and night.
Rose Atoll stands, untouched, alone,
A fragile world the ocean has grown,
With birds that soar and turtles that glide,
And secrets beneath the tide.
The government watches, careful and wise,
Protecting its beauty from greedy eyes,
They guard the land, the reefs, the shore,
So it can stay forevermore.
But some see money in its island,
They dream of fishing boats a plenty,
Turning peace into a crowded place,
Erasing nature without a trace.
I cannot stand and just agree,
That profit should rule land and sea,
For once it’s gone, we can’t restore,
The life that lived on that quiet shore.
Let Rose Atoll remain the same,
Not just a place, but more than a name,
A symbol of what we choose to save
Not everything is ours to pave.
Section:
[Local News](/local-news)
Tags:
[ROSE ATOLL](/rose-atoll)

item.title: Trump Homeland nominee Markwayne Mullin advances to vote before full Senate
item.description: WASHINGTON — A U.S. Senate committee voted on Thursday to advance the nomination of Senator Markwayne Mullin to become homeland security secretary, even as the committee’s Republican chairman criticized Mullin and voted against him.

item.title: Trump-appointed arts panel approves gold coin featuring president’s image
item.description: WASHINGTON — A federal arts panel comprised of members appointed by Donald Trump on Thursday unanimously approved a commemorative gold coin featuring his image, part of a series of coins the U.S. Mint is planning to produce to celebrate America’s 250th birthday this year. The 24-carat gold coin is the latest effort by Trump and his allies to put the president’s name on buildings, government programs and U.S. currency since his second White House term began in January 2025.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — A federal arts panel comprised of members appointed by Donald Trump on Thursday unanimously approved a commemorative gold coin featuring his image, part of a series of coins the U.S. Mint is planning to produce to celebrate America’s 250th birthday this year. The 24-carat gold coin is the latest effort by Trump and his allies to put the president’s name on buildings, government programs and U.S. currency since his second White House term began in January 2025.

item.title: Obituaries for March 20
item.description: Dennis Kin Wah Lee Sr., 80, of Hilo died Feb. 20 at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. Born in Honokaa Hospital, he was retired from the county Hawaii. He was an engineering consultant at Wesley Segawa and Associates, former county Public Works director and retired from the county Department of Water Supply. Visitation 4-6 p.m. Friday, March 27, at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Memorial service at 6 p.m. Survived by sons, Dennis Kin Wah Lee Jr. of Las Vegas, Bruce (JoVonne) Lee Sr. and David Lee of Hilo, Chris (Donna) Lee of Las Vegas; siblings, Robert You Wah (Sandra) Lee Jr. and Sharon Ann Raniada of Hilo; sister-in-law, Suzanne Lee of Hilo; eight grandchildren and seven great-grandchildren; cousins, nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.
item.source:markdown: Dennis Kin Wah Lee Sr., 80, of Hilo died Feb. 20 at Hilo Benioff Medical Center. Born in Honokaa Hospital, he was retired from the county Hawaii. He was an engineering consultant at Wesley Segawa and Associates, former county Public Works director and retired from the county Department of Water Supply. Visitation 4-6 p.m. Friday, March 27, at Dodo Mortuary Chapel. Memorial service at 6 p.m. Survived by sons, Dennis Kin Wah Lee Jr. of Las Vegas, Bruce (JoVonne) Lee Sr. and David Lee of Hilo, Chris (Donna) Lee of Las Vegas; siblings, Robert You Wah (Sandra) Lee Jr. and Sharon Ann Raniada of Hilo; sister-in-law, Suzanne Lee of Hilo; eight grandchildren and seven great-grandchildren; cousins, nieces and nephews. Arrangements by Dodo Mortuary.

item.title: Student loan office to leave the Education Department

item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — The Trump administration on Thursday announced plans to shift the federal government’s $1.7 trillion student loan portfolio to the Treasury Department, the latest in a series of bureaucratic moves aimed at dismantling the Education Department.

item.title: Judge ends asylum claim of Minnesotan boy detained by ICE, report says
item.description: NEW YORK — An immigration judge has ended the asylum claim of the Ecuadorean boy who was detained during an immigration raid in Minneapolis, Minnesota Public Radio reported on Wednesday, citing a lawyer for the boy and his family. Liam Conejo Ramos was taken into custody at the age of five along with his father in late January and spent 10 days in a detention facility in Texas before both of them were released and returned home. A photograph showing Liam in a blue bunny hat outside his house, with federal agents standing nearby, drew national attention.
item.source:markdown: NEW YORK — An immigration judge has ended the asylum claim of the Ecuadorean boy who was detained during an immigration raid in Minneapolis, Minnesota Public Radio reported on Wednesday, citing a lawyer for the boy and his family. Liam Conejo Ramos was taken into custody at the age of five along with his father in late January and spent 10 days in a detention facility in Texas before both of them were released and returned home. A photograph showing Liam in a blue bunny hat outside his house, with federal agents standing nearby, drew national attention.

item.title: Americans believe Trump will send troops into Iran, and don’t like the idea, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description: WASHINGTON — Some 65% of Americans believe U.S. President Donald Trump will order troops into a large-scale ground war in Iran and just 7% support that idea, a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Thursday found.
item.source:markdown: WASHINGTON — Some 65% of Americans believe U.S. President Donald Trump will order troops into a large-scale ground war in Iran and just 7% support that idea, a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Thursday found.

item.title: China cracks down on fentanyl networks after US pressure

item.source:markdown: BEIJING — China has arrested seven people and subjected 12 more to “criminal compulsory measures” in a campaign targeting traffickers in fentanyl precursor chemicals, state media said on Thursday, following pressure from the United States.

item.description:

item.title: Captain Sam and his crew arrived in Yap
item.description: Friday, March 20, 2026, /// DYCA PIO Yap FM - Captain Sam and his crew departed the Yap Fishing Authority port on March 13th at 6:00 pm and arrived early Thursday morning, March 19th, at 6:00 am with over 2,000 pounds of fresh pelagic and bottom reef fish. Pelagic fish, including: Mahi-Mahi (dabar), Barracuda ( mal’), Rainbow Runner Fish (fofow), and Dogtooth Tuna ( yasul) Bottom/reef fish, including: Humpback Snapper (goychab), Grouper (sabak’uw), Emperor Fish (rogrog), Uku Snapper Fish...

item.title: ‘Syntax of Steel’ merges CHamoru heritage, modern design
